flashcards.front: What is a frame in virtual memory systems?
flashcards.back: A frame is a fixed-size block of physical memory in a paging system. It is the counterpart to a page (fixed-size block of logical memory), and both have the same size.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between equal allocation and proportional allocation of frames?
flashcards.back: Equal allocation gives each process the same number of frames regardless of size. Proportional allocation allocates frames based on process size or priority.
Global replacement allows a process to select a replacement frame from the set of all frames in the system, even if the frame is currently allocated to another process.
Local replacement restricts each process to selecting only from the frames currently allocated to that process.

flashcards.front: What is the working set model in frame allocation?
flashcards.back: The working set model maintains the pages that a process is actively using in its frames. It allocates enough frames to keep the process within its working set to minimize page faults.

flashcards.front: What is page fault frequency (PFF) in frame allocation?
flashcards.back: PFF measures the rate of page faults. The system adjusts frame allocation based on whether the fault rate is above or below a threshold - allocating more frames if too many faults occur.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between degree of multiprogramming and frame allocation?
flashcards.back: As the degree of multiprogramming increases, the average number of frames per process decreases, potentially increasing page faults. The system must balance process count with available frames.

flashcards.front: What is fixed priority allocation of frames?
flashcards.back: Each process is assigned a fixed priority based on which more frames are allocated to higher-priority processes, while lower-priority processes receive fewer frames.

flashcards.front: What happens when a process receives fewer frames than its working set size?
flashcards.back: The process will experience thrashing - excessive page faults that severely degrade performance as the process constantly swaps pages in and out of memory.

flashcards.front: What is proportional allocation based on process priority?
flashcards.back: Frame allocation is calculated as: frames for process i = (priority of i / sum of priorities) × available frames. Higher priority processes get more frames.

flashcards.front: What are the two main approaches to frame allocation?
flashcards.back: The two main approaches are: 1) Fixed allocation (each process gets a fixed number of frames), and 2) Variable allocation (number of frames changes during execution).

flashcards.front: What is the minimum number of frames required for a process?
flashcards.back: The minimum number of frames equals the number of pages required to hold a single instruction, including all memory operands it references. This is determined by the machine architecture.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the allocation algorithm in virtual memory?
flashcards.back: The allocation algorithm determines how physical memory frames are distributed among competing processes to optimize CPU utilization and minimize page faults.

flashcards.front: What is variable allocation with local replacement?
flashcards.back: Each process starts with some frames and can gain or lose frames over time. When replacement is needed, it chooses from its own allocated frames.

flashcards.front: What is variable allocation with global replacement?
flashcards.back: Processes can select replacement frames from all frames in the system. This makes frame allocation more dynamic but can cause one process to steal frames from another.
